---
title: "Plant-Based vs. Meat-Based Fast Food: Yelp Review Sentiment Analysis"
---

A dataset of 3,915 Yelp reviews comparing consumer sentiment toward plant-based fast food menu items (e.g., "Impossible," "Beyond," "Sofritas") versus traditional meat-based controls (e.g., "Whopper," "Carnitas") within the same restaurant chains, spanning 7 fast-food chains and hundreds of unique locations. Reviews were scored using transformer-based sentiment analysis (RoBERTa) for sentiment valence, and a large language model extraction procedure flagged mentions of sensory attributes, texture, and environmental/ethical motivations. Using fixed-effects and linear mixed models to control for restaurant, user, and temporal heterogeneity, plant-based items were found to receive significantly higher star ratings (β=0.73) and more positive sentiment valence (β=0.33) than meat-based controls, an effect driven primarily by moral utility rather than superior sensory performance. Ideal for teaching mixed-effects models, text/sentiment data, and analysis of nested/clustered review data (reviews nested within users and within restaurant locations).


## Source and Citation

- **Citation**: Healey, P.M. (2026). Plant-based vs. meat-based: a comparative sentiment analysis of consumer reviews in fast food settings. Food Quality and Preference, 144, 105995. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodqual.2026.105995
- **URL**: [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0950329326001497](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0950329326001497)
- **License**: See publisher for reuse terms

## Dataset Characteristics

- **Observations**: 3915
- **Variables**: 24
- **Missingness present**: FALSE
- **Longitudinal**: TRUE
- **Hierarchical**: TRUE

## Variable Dictionary

| Variable | Type | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| review_id | categorical | A unique alphanumeric identifier assigned by Yelp to each individual review observation |
| user_id | categorical | A unique identifier for the Yelp user, used as a random intercept in mixed-effects models to control for individual reviewer stringency or bias |
| business_id | categorical | A unique identifier for the specific restaurant location, used to cluster standard errors and as a random intercept to account for store-level quality |
| chain_key | categorical | A standardized, case-insensitive categorical variable used as a fixed effect to represent the parent restaurant franchise |
| chain_name | categorical | The descriptive name of the restaurant chain (e.g., Burger King, Chipotle) |
| city | categorical | The geographic city of the restaurant location, used as a high-dimensional fixed effect to control for local market heterogeneity |
| state | spatial | The US state where the restaurant business is registered in the Yelp metadata |
| stars | ordinal | The primary dependent variable: consumer's ordinal rating of the experience on a 1-to-5 scale |
| date | date_time | Timestamp for the review (study window: January 1, 2010 to December 31, 2021) |
| text | text | The raw prose of the review used for hierarchical keyword classification and transformer-based sentiment inference |
| useful | count | Count of other users who voted the review as 'useful' |
| funny | count | Count of other users who voted the review as 'funny' |
| cool | count | Count of other users who voted the review as 'cool' |
| item_label | categorical | Categorical assignment identifying whether a review belongs to the plant-based 'target' group or the meat-based 'control' group |
| target_canonical | categorical | The specific plant-based term (e.g., 'impossible,' 'sofritas') detected in the text that triggered the target classification |
| control_canonical | categorical | The specific meat-based term (e.g., 'chicken,' 'steak') detected when no target keywords were present |
| sent_label | categorical | Discrete categorical sentiment output (Positive, Negative, or Neutral) from the RoBERTa sentiment classification model |
| sent_score | continuous | Probability or confidence level associated with the sentiment label, ranging from 0 to 1 |
| sent_valence | continuous | Continuous sentiment metric derived from the label and score, ranging from -1.0 to +1.0; the primary textual dependent variable |
| sensory_mention | binary | Binary flag indicating presence of text related to physical food attributes (e.g., taste, flavor) |
| texture_specific | binary | Binary flag identifying reviews that specifically discuss texture or mouthfeel (e.g., crispy, rubbery) |
| environmental_mention | binary | Binary flag indicating mentions of sustainability, animal welfare, or ethical motivations; used to test the moral utility hypothesis |
| target | binary | Binary numeric representation of item_label (1 = Target/plant-based, 0 = Control/meat-based), used as the primary independent variable |
| month | date_time | Month and year of the review, used to control for seasonal shocks and longitudinal trends |

## Suggested Learning Objectives

- Fit a mixed-effects model with crossed random intercepts for user and restaurant location
- Use fixed effects to control for chain, geography, and time
- Work with text-derived sentiment scores as a continuous outcome variable
- Test a mediation-style hypothesis: does an environmental mention explain the rating difference between plant-based and meat-based items?

## Suggested Classroom Activities

- Compare a naive regression of stars on item_label to a mixed-effects model accounting for user and restaurant clustering
- Explore whether the plant-based rating advantage holds across different chains
- Use environmental_mention and sensory_mention as predictors to test competing explanations for the rating gap

## Common Student Misconceptions

- Treating reviews from the same user or restaurant as independent observations
- Assuming sentiment_score and sentiment_valence measure the same thing without checking how they are constructed
- Interpreting a positive association between plant-based items and ratings as evidence of superior taste, without considering the environmental halo explanation

## Load This Dataset in R

```r
library(statsdata)
data <- load_dataset("plantbased-meat-reviews")
```
